item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was very much on board until the ending, I'm a sucker for any kind of "creature feature" SCP. But it feels like the end of a campfire ghost story, a sort of "and now he's coming… for YOU!" type of thing. I think a better source of creepiness would be some elaboration on how this thing interacts with humans, maybe an exploration log?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked it, and upvoted, but I definitely feel like the end is missing something. It seems strange that, in-universe, an SCP file would end on a super threatening note without also recording what the Foundation's response is. I get that the Containment Procedures give instructions on what to do when the coral is sighted, but did they send out any search-and-destroy teams, or begin emergency monitoring of nearby waters? Any evacuations in place for civilian vessels?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I mostly agree with Roget. While the last addendum achieves the weird/wacky effect that, as I believe, was the point, the overall article still feels like most of the skip parts that could've been interesting are hidden from us for no good reason.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don’t really find the assimilation angle to be developed enough to be scary, it’s demonstrated an ability to adapt to machines and move but it’s not super clear where it’s going or what the stakes would be. It’s telekinetic but we don’t really get any characterization of it so we don’t have anything to go on for what motivates this entity. The stakes of what the consequences for further assimilation could be aren’t clearly defined. To me it’s also not really scary imagery on its own, although I can see how others could find it unnerving. The last line is pretty good and could serve as a climactic moment in a more polished version of this article.

apologies for curtness I was half asleep

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the best thing about this is the implications of the last part; It can absorb and assimilate drones and technology.
Isn't it scary to think about what else it could assimilate?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a really weak paint-by-numbers article. Unless there’s something I’m not getting, it’s a coral reef that has hive-mind and telekinetic properties which is able to absorb and/or adapt to anything it has assimilated.

Getting my pet gripes out of the way: Every paragraph starting with the numerical designation is always a pet peeve of mine, and the containment procedures are quite generic and don’t really do justice to the Keter designation. Something that takes enormous resources to contain and is extremely dangerous deserves a little more than a measly paragraph that boils down to “watch it from space and shoot torpedos if it moves.”

That being said, my specific issue with this piece is that it really lacks imagination and detail. There’s an expansive reef with supposedly diverse wildlife but the only details we got are that it turns purple/orange when it’s distressed and there are starfish and plankton.

There’s so much more you could do there! Then there’s things like the divers being communicated with telepathically that never gets elaborated on.

The worst of it is that at the very end we get some of that weird unique detail when it grows legs and walks off but it’s confined to a mere sentence and is so far out of left field that it leaves the reader bewildered.

I’m certain this idea could be done well but this is mediocre at best. It’s starved of anything unique that would truly make it memorable. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
God the fridge logic here is so good. Great stuff wery!

+ Spoilers-ish?
- Spoilers

Both the implications that this thing has already absorbed people (since it can mind control divers who get close), and the implications that it picked itself up and walked towards the Australian mainland are great, and are what sells this for me.

item.title: Re: Coral see coral do.
item.content:encoded: I read it as the drones being pissed and taking control somehow.

item.title: Coral see coral do.
item.content:encoded: Great as ever. A welcome addition to the skips that involve the ocean getting like, really fucking pissed.

Thanks to all my mad conspirators both online and IRL who encouraged this.

Big thanks to wmaitla, The Great Hippo, TheMightyMcB, and Popsioak for looking at the draft.

(Thanks to the nerds at the Queensland University of Technology for making our world just a little bit more fun, coral-y, and crazy.)

Picture is CC 2.0 source here: https://commons.m.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Crown-of-thorns_starfish_Acanthaster_planci_(7569625822).jpg
